title: Conduct & Culture
subtitle: Beyond the letter of the law

hook.metaphor: You can obey every written rule of a game and still be the player everyone hates — the one who games the offside trap, time-wastes, and bends every grey area. Compliance has the same trap: you can be technically legal and still be doing the wrong thing. That gap between 'legal' and 'right' is where **conduct** lives.
hook.scenario: A bank sells a product that is fully legal and fully disclosed (in 40 pages of fine print) — but it's designed so most customers will lose money and never understand why. No rule is broken. Is the bank in the clear?

learn: **Conduct risk** is the risk of treating customers, markets, or competitors unfairly — even within the letter of the law. Regulators increasingly judge firms on **outcomes for customers**, not just box-ticking. 'It was technically legal' is no longer a safe defence (our fine-print bank is very much *not* in the clear).

This shows up as duties like **Treating Customers Fairly** and, in the UK, the **Consumer Duty** — and in Australia, ASIC's focus on fair dealing and product **design & distribution obligations**. The thread: products should do right by the people who buy them, not just survive a legal reading.

Two everyday conduct hazards: **conflicts of interest** (when what's good for you is bad for the customer — e.g. pushing a product because it pays you more) must be identified and managed; and **ethics** beyond the rules — the daily judgement calls no rulebook covers.

And the safety valve for all of it is **speak-up culture / whistleblowing.** People must be able to raise concerns without fear, and protected by law when they do. A firm where bad conduct can't be safely reported is a firm flying blind — which loops straight back to **tone at the top**: leaders decide whether 'is this *right*?' is a question people feel free to ask.
bridge: So at work, you can apply a test the rulebook can't: beyond 'is this legal?', ask **'is this fair to the customer, and would it survive being on the front page?'** Watch for conflicts of interest, and make sure people around you feel safe to speak up. Conduct is where compliance stops being paperwork and becomes integrity.

interactions.title: Legal but wrong?
interactions.prompt: The bank's product is legal and disclosed, but designed so most customers quietly lose. Verdict?

options.label: Fine — no rule was broken.
options.feedback: This is exactly the conduct trap. Regulators now judge customer *outcomes*, not just legality. 'Technically legal' won't save you from a conduct finding, a fine, or front-page damage.

options.label: A conduct failure — it's unfair to customers despite being legal.
options.feedback: Right. Conduct risk lives in the gap between 'legal' and 'right'. A product engineered for customers to lose fails the fairness test no matter how thick the disclosure.

options.label: Acceptable as long as the fine print is technically complete.
options.feedback: Burying the truth in 40 pages is a classic unfair-by-design pattern. Disclosure isn't a licence to mislead — the outcome still has to be fair.

interactions.title: Conduct: good or hazard?
interactions.prompt: Sort each behaviour by whether it reflects good conduct or a conduct hazard.

items.label: Recommending the product that's best for the customer
Pushing a product because it pays you the biggest bonus
Making key terms genuinely clear and prominent
Burying the catch in 40 pages of fine print
Protecting a colleague who raised a concern

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Conduct is the gap between 'legal' and 'right' — regulators now judge whether customers are treated fairly, so the real test is 'would this survive the front page?', backed by a culture where people can speak up.”

options: The risk of breaking an explicit written rule only
The risk of treating customers/markets unfairly, even within the law
The risk of a system outage
The risk of a market crash
checkpoint.explain: It lives in the gap between legal and right — regulators judge customer outcomes, not just technical legality.

checkpoint.q: A conflict of interest must be…
checkpoint.explain: When your gain harms the customer, the conflict has to be surfaced and managed, not concealed.

checkpoint.q: A healthy speak-up / whistleblowing culture matters because…
options: Without safe reporting, bad conduct stays hidden and the firm flies blind
checkpoint.explain: Protected, fearless reporting is how problems surface — tone at the top decides whether people dare to ask 'is this right?'
